stories.title: John Deere Classic Final Round — Glover and Hodges in a Sunday Shootout

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text:
Lucas Glover and Lee Hodges are tied at 16-under heading into the final round of the John Deere Classic at TPC Deere Run.

$1.584M and 500 FedExCup points on the line. Who takes it? #PGATour #JohnDeereClassic

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text:
Zac Blair, Ben Kohles and Jackson Suber sit one shot back at 15-under as the final round of the #JohnDeereClassic heats up.

Glover and Hodges lead the way. Sunday at TPC Deere Run — chaos incoming. #PGATour

facebook.long_form:
⛳ SUNDAY SHOOTOUT: WHO WINS THE JOHN DEERE CLASSIC?

It comes down to today at TPC Deere Run in Silvis, Illinois. Lucas Glover and Lee Hodges enter the final round of the John Deere Classic tied at 16-under par — co-leaders going into one of the most wide-open Sunday finales of the PGA Tour season.

The backstory makes it even richer. Glover, one of the most experienced players in the field, put together a remarkable 36-hole run — shooting 63 and 65 in the first two rounds with zero bogeys — before a 69 on Saturday kept him at the top. He offset any Saturday stumbles with an eagle chip-in and late birdies. His consistency over 54 holes is a testament to a veteran who knows how to be in this position.

Hodges had a different kind of Saturday. He surged with four birdies in a six-hole stretch on the back nine in the third round, shooting a 67 to erase a two-shot deficit and join Glover at the top. He comes into Sunday with genuine momentum.

One shot back and very much in contention: Zac Blair, Ben Kohles, and Jackson Suber all sit at 15-under. With three players one shot back, this is not a two-man race — it is a full shootout.

The stakes: $1.584 million to the winner. 500 FedExCup points. And the right to call yourself the 2026 John Deere Classic champion.

Who do you have? Drop your pick in the comments!
facebook.image_caption: Glover and Hodges tied at 16-under. Three more players one back at 15-under. Sunday at TPC Deere Run is going to be electric. ⛳ #PGATour #JohnDeereClassic

article.headline: John Deere Classic Final Round: Glover and Hodges Share the Lead at 16-Under in a Sunday Shootout

research.facts:
John Deere Classic Final Round
**Key Facts (HIGH confidence — multiple sources):**
- Tournament: John Deere Classic at TPC Deere Run, Silvis, IL (July 2-5, 2026)
- Purse: $8.8 million; winner's share: $1.584 million; 500 FedExCup points
- Defending champion: Brian Campbell (won 2025 in a playoff over Emiliano Grillo)
- 54-hole co-leaders entering final round: Lucas Glover (-16) and Lee Hodges (-16)
- Glover's R3: 69 (eagle chip-in + birdies; had zero bogeys through 36 holes); 54-hole R1-R2-R3: 63-65-69
- Hodges' R3: shot 67 Saturday with four birdies in a six-hole stretch on the back nine to catch Glover
- Other players entering final round: Zac Blair (-15), Ben Kohles (-15), Jackson Suber (-15)
- Zach Johnson at -13, Preston Stout at -12 entering Sunday
- Jordan Spieth made the cut and is in the field for Sunday
- Jackson Koivun (highly anticipated pro debut) missed the cut
- Scottie Scheffler (world No. 1) sitting out the JDC, leads FedExCup with 3,511 pts
- Viktor Hovland entered as defending Travelers champ (-21, beat Scheffler, earned $3.6M, 700 FedExCup pts)
**Sources:** NBC Sports, ESPN, CBS Sports, PGA Tour R1/R2 recaps

stories.title: Zach Johnson's Hometown Stand — Iowa's 50-Year-Old Skipped Three Majors for This

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text:
Zach Johnson is 50 years old. He skipped three majors to play his home tournament. He is three shots off the lead in the final round of the #JohnDeereClassic.

Iowa does not forget its own. #PGATour

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text:
At 50, Zach Johnson chose the John Deere Classic over three majors this year.

He skipped tournaments other players dream about — for the chance to compete in Iowa.

That's what home means. #JohnDeereClassic #PGATour

facebook.long_form:
🏠 50 YEARS OLD. SKIPPED THREE MAJORS. FIGHTING IN HIS HOME STATE.

There are players who play every event to maximize ranking points and earnings. And then there is Zach Johnson.

This year, Johnson, at 50 years old, made a choice that speaks to something the statistics do not capture: he skipped three major championships to play the John Deere Classic. Not to rest. Not to prepare. To play in Iowa. His Iowa.

Johnson was born and raised in Cedar Rapids, Iowa — less than two hours from TPC Deere Run in Silvis. He has won this tournament before. He knows the course, the crowds, the way summer feels in the Midwest. When the John Deere Classic is on the schedule, Zach Johnson shows up, regardless of what else is happening in the golf world.

The results say it was the right call. Johnson is three shots off the lead entering the final round, sitting at 13-under par. He shot a 66 in Saturday's third round — a reminder that even at 50, the 2007 Masters and Open Championship champion still belongs in the conversation on any given Sunday.

The Silverstone grandstands erupt for Hamilton. But in Iowa, the galleries erupt for Zach Johnson.

If Johnson wins today, it would be one of the most compelling storylines of the 2026 PGA Tour season.

Are you rooting for Zach Johnson on Sunday? Let us know.
facebook.image_caption: 50 years old. Skipped three majors. Fighting in his home state. Zach Johnson at the John Deere Classic is must-watch Sunday golf. 🏠 #PGATour

article.headline: Zach Johnson's Iowa Stand: At 50, He Skipped Three Majors to Compete at Home

stories.title: Preston Stout — World No. 1 Amateur Contends on Sponsor Exemption

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text:
Preston Stout is the world No. 1 amateur — the Oklahoma State NCAA champion — and he's in contention at the #JohnDeereClassic on a sponsor exemption.

Four shots back entering the final round. The kid has something to say. #PGATour

facebook.long_form:
🎓 WORLD No. 1 AMATEUR CRASHES THE PGA TOUR PARTY AT THE JOHN DEERE CLASSIC

The name to know from this week's John Deere Classic is Preston Stout.

Stout is the reigning Oklahoma State NCAA champion and the world's No. 1 ranked amateur golfer. At the John Deere Classic on a sponsor exemption — one of the coveted invitations that gives college players or non-exempt players access to Tour events — he has not just competed. He has contended.

At 12-under par entering the final round, Stout sits four shots off the pace set by co-leaders Lucas Glover and Lee Hodges. For a college golfer in what is essentially a glorified audition, that is an extraordinary performance. The leaderboard does not care about resumes, and Stout's is sitting right there alongside PGA Tour veterans who have been competing at this level for years.

The John Deere Classic has a tradition of featuring amateur talent. This week, the world's best amateur has proven he belongs on the same course, in the same conditions, against the same players.

Stout's performance will not go unnoticed by PGA Tour officials, equipment companies, or fans watching the development of the next generation of professional golf.

What do you think — is Preston Stout ready to turn professional? Tell us in the comments.
facebook.image_caption: The world No. 1 amateur — Oklahoma State NCAA champion Preston Stout — is 4 shots off the lead at the John Deere Classic on a sponsor exemption. The kid can play. ⛳ #PGATour

article.headline: Preston Stout Is in Contention at the John Deere Classic. The World Is Paying Attention.

stories.title: LIV Golf Financial Crisis — Saudi Funding Shortfall Threatens Season

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
LIV Golf's CEO won't guarantee the final 4 events of 2026 will be played.

Saudi Arabia's PIF has reportedly provided ~$200M of the ~$600M committed for the season.

The gap is real. The future is uncertain. #LIVGolf #Golf

facebook.long_form:
⚠️ LIV GOLF'S FINANCIAL CRISIS: THE TOUR THAT MAY NOT FINISH ITS SEASON

The story behind the story in professional golf right now has nothing to do with leaderboards or scoring averages. It is about money — specifically, the $400 million that LIV Golf says it is owed by its primary financial backer, Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund.

LIV Golf CEO Scott O'Neil sat down for an interview in June and, in a moment of remarkable candor, acknowledged that he could not guarantee the remaining four events of the 2026 season would be played. The math is stark: PIF committed approximately $600 million for the 2026 LIV season. As of early July, only approximately $200 million has been provided — roughly $66 million in May and $130 million in June. That leaves a gap of roughly $400 million, money needed to pay player salaries and appearance fees, tournament operations, prize purses, travel, and the rest of what it costs to run a global golf tour.

In April, PIF stated publicly that substantial long-term investment in LIV "is no longer consistent with the current phase of PIF's investment strategy." That statement, quiet and bureaucratic in tone, carried enormous implications.

The events now in question include the UK event scheduled for July 23-26 at JCB Golf & Country Club, plus events in Indianapolis and Michigan. The New York event in early August is considered the most likely to survive.

For LIV players — many of whom signed multi-year guaranteed contracts worth tens of millions of dollars — the situation is deeply uncertain. For the sport of golf, LIV's potential implosion would represent a seismic shift in the professional landscape that began in 2022.

What happens to LIV Golf's players if the tour cannot finish its season? Sound off in the comments.
facebook.image_caption: LIV Golf's CEO won't guarantee the final 4 events of 2026. The Saudi PIF funding gap is reportedly $400M. The tour's future is genuinely uncertain. #LIVGolf

article.headline: LIV Golf's Financial Crisis: Saudi Funding Shortfall Puts the 2026 Season at Risk

research.facts:
LIV Golf Financial Crisis
**Key Facts (HIGH confidence — multiple sources):**
- Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund (PIF) committed ~$600M for 2026 LIV season
- PIF has reportedly provided only ~$200M: ~$66M in May, ~$130M in June
- LIV owed approximately $400M more to cover player contracts, operations, purses
- CEO Scott O'Neil publicly refused to guarantee the remaining 4 events (interview June 9)
- Events at risk: UK (July 23-26, JCB Golf & Country Club), Indianapolis, Michigan
- New York event (early August) considered most likely to survive
- PIF stated in April: substantial long-term LIV investment "no longer consistent with current phase" of PIF strategy
- Most recent completed LIV events: Tyrrell Hatton won LIV Andalucia at Valderrama; Lucas Herbert won LIV Virginia (Trump National D.C.)
- Anthony Kim shot 62 (-10) bogey-free in Virginia final round for 4Aces
**Sources:** Forbes/ESPN, Golf Digest, Golfmagic, OutKick/FoxNews

stories.title: The Open Championship at Royal Birkdale — 11 Days Away

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
The Open Championship at Royal Birkdale is 11 days away.

Scottie Scheffler returns the Claret Jug. Every major name in golf is in the field.

A new Last Chance Qualifier in 2026 adds one final spot. It's almost time. #TheOpen #Golf

facebook.long_form:
🏆 THE OPEN IS 11 DAYS AWAY — ROYAL BIRKDALE IS READY

July 16 is circled. The 154th Open Championship tees off at Royal Birkdale in Southport, England — and the golf world is counting down.

This is the 11th time Royal Birkdale has hosted The Open, and the R&A is projecting record attendance of more than 300,000 spectators over the week. Birkdale, with its characteristic willow scrub-lined fairways and demanding coastal conditions, is one of the most beloved venues in links golf.

Scottie Scheffler arrives as defending champion, having won the 2025 Open at Royal Portrush. Before the tournament begins, Scheffler will participate in the Heroes Classic on Tuesday, July 14 — a pre-Open charity event — and officially return the Claret Jug ahead of the week. He leads the world rankings and the FedExCup standings, and arrives at Birkdale in the form of a true champion.

The field is loaded. Rory McIlroy, Xander Schauffele, Viktor Hovland, and virtually every major name in men's professional golf is entered or expected. Several LIV Golf players qualified through Final Qualifying in June — including Josele Ballester, Caleb Surratt, and Peter Uihlein — with additional spots available through the Genesis Scottish Open (July 9-12) and the brand-new Last Chance Qualifier, held at Royal Birkdale itself on the Monday before play begins.

The Genesis Scottish Open at The Renaissance Club in North Berwick runs July 9 to 12, one week before Birkdale, and serves as both an elite tune-up and an Open qualifier for the top non-exempt finisher. Scheffler, McIlroy, and Hovland are among those entered.

Eleven days. One Claret Jug. Who wins at Royal Birkdale?
facebook.image_caption: The Open Championship at Royal Birkdale is 11 days away. Scottie Scheffler defends. Every major name in golf is coming. Who wins? 🏆 #TheOpen #Golf

article.headline: The Open Championship at Royal Birkdale Is 11 Days Away — Here's Everything You Need to Know

research.facts:
The Open Championship at Royal Birkdale
**Key Facts (HIGH confidence — multiple sources):**
- 154th Open Championship: Royal Birkdale, Southport, England; July 16-19, 2026
- 11th time at Royal Birkdale hosting The Open
- Defending champion: Scottie Scheffler (won 2025 at Royal Portrush)
- Scheffler will participate in Heroes Classic (Tuesday July 14) before returning Claret Jug
- 132-player field; 113 spots committed as of early July; 20 more via Final Qualifying (June 30, four sites)
- Notable qualifiers: Scheffler, McIlroy, Schauffele, Hovland, Harman, Henley, DeChambeau, Clark, Fitzpatrick, Robert MacIntyre, Corey Conners, Gotterup, Haotong Li
- LIV qualifiers via Final Qualifying: Josele Ballester, Caleb Surratt, Peter Uihlein
- New for 2026: Last Chance Qualifier (12 golfers compete at Birkdale on Monday of Open week for 1 final spot)
- Remaining qualification routes: top 5 Race to Dubai after BMW International Open (non-exempt); top non-exempt finisher at Genesis Scottish Open (July 9-12)
- Genesis Scottish Open (July 9-12, The Renaissance Club, North Berwick): Co-sanctioned PGA/DP World; Scheffler, McIlroy, Hovland headline; serves as Open qualifying series event
- R&A projects 300K+ attendance record at Birkdale
**Sources:** Golf Digest, Golf Channel, PGA Tour Final Qualifying, The Open official site
